items.q: What is the difference between a solar and a lunar eclipse?
items.correct: In a solar eclipse the Moon hides the Sun; in a lunar eclipse Earth's shadow falls on the Moon.
items.random_wrong: A solar eclipse is when the Moon simply disappears.
items.why: Solar eclipses need the Moon between Sun and Earth; lunar eclipses need Earth between Sun and Moon.

items.q: Does the Moon produce its own light?
items.correct: No - it reflects sunlight, and phases show how much of the lit half we can see.
items.misconception: The Moon glows with its own light.
items.why: We see the sunlit portion of the Moon; the changing angle, not Earth's shadow, gives the phases.

items.q: Are Moon phases caused by Earth's shadow?
items.correct: No - phases depend on the Moon's position relative to the Sun and Earth, not Earth's shadow.
items.misconception: Yes, the phases are Earth's shadow on the Moon.
items.random_wrong: Clouds passing over cause the phases.
items.why: Earth's shadow only causes lunar eclipses; phases come from which part of the lit Moon faces us.

items.q: Approximate length of a synodic month?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 29.5 days. “27.3 days” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Phase when the lit side faces away from Earth?
items.why: It's New Moon. “Full Moon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days in a synodic month (rounded)?
items.why: The real figure is 30 days. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
